prompt:
Fleet-agent ticket. Every terminal in our stores keeps one long-lived uplink to the store hub, and the link-management code has been rewritten by three teams: one reconnects forever with no cap, one loses queued scans on reconnect, one accepts writes while shutting down. The root problem is that the lifecycle rules live tangled up with sockets and timers, so nobody can test them. I want the rules extracted into one pure module, connfsm.ts — no npm, node 26 with type stripping (no enums, no namespaces, no parameter properties, explicit .ts import extensions). test_connfsm.ts is the acceptance contract: `node --test test_connfsm.ts` green means done, test file read-only.

Seed-ledger note: nearest neighbors are ts-optimistic-store (pending-MUTATION bookkeeping with confirm/reject reconciliation; here the queue is one incidental FIFO and the substance is the per-state legal-event table and backoff transitions) and go-circuit-breaker (consecutive-failure call breaker around fn() with an injectable clock; here there are no wrapped calls and no clock at all — the host owns sockets and timers and feeds events in).

Design: the machine is synchronous and pure. The host calls one method per event; each call either throws a typed rejection or transitions and returns an Effect[] the host must act on. Effects: {kind:'dial', attempt} (open the transport), {kind:'send', ops} (write these ops, in order), {kind:'wait', delayMs} (start a retry timer; when it fires call timer()), {kind:'discard', ops} (these queued ops will never be sent — surface that).

export class ConnectionMachine, constructed with {backoff, maxAttempts, maxQueue}: backoff = non-empty array of positive retry delays in ms; maxAttempts >= 1 = consecutive failed dials before giving up; maxQueue >= 1. Bad options throw RangeError. States: 'idle' | 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'reconnecting' | 'draining' | 'closed', exposed as .state; pending() returns a COPY of the queued ops.

Event methods and the legal-event table (an event not listed for the current state throws IllegalEventError carrying .state, .event and .legal = the sorted list below, with state and event named in the message; a rejected event changes NOTHING — state, queue, counters):
- idle: close, connect
- connecting: close, down, enqueue, up
- connected: close, down, drain, enqueue
- reconnecting: close, enqueue, timer
- draining: close, down
- closed: nothing — terminal; even close() throws there, legal []

Semantics:
- connect(): idle -> connecting, effects [dial attempt 1].
- enqueue(op): connected -> effect [send [op]] immediately (nothing queues). connecting/reconnecting -> op joins the FIFO queue, no effects; if the queue already holds maxQueue ops throw QueueFullError (carries .limit) and queue nothing.
- up(): connecting -> connected. If ops are queued, exactly one effect [send ops-in-FIFO-order] and the queue empties; else no effects. A successful connection also ends the current failure run (see down).
- down(reason): connecting -> this dial FAILED. Count consecutive failed dials since the last successful connection; at maxAttempts give up: -> closed, effects [discard queuedOps] if any are queued, else []. Below the cap: -> reconnecting, effects [wait backoff[failures-1]], where a failure run longer than the schedule keeps using its LAST delay. connected -> the established link dropped: failure count resets to zero, -> reconnecting, effects [wait backoff[0]]. draining -> orderly confirmation: -> closed, no effects.
- timer(): reconnecting -> connecting, effects [dial attempt failures+1] (so the first retry after an established-link drop is attempt 1 again).
- drain(): connected -> draining, no effects (the host initiated it, it knows); draining accepts no ops and waits for down.
- close(): any non-closed state -> closed; if ops are queued the single effect [discard ops], else no effects.

Worked example, backoff [100,200,400], maxAttempts 4, from idle: connect->dial#1, down->wait 100, timer->dial#2, down->wait 200, timer->dial#3, down->wait 400, timer->dial#4, down->closed (gave up). Variant: ...timer->dial#2, up (connected), down->wait 100 (schedule reset), timer->dial#1.

No Date.now, no setTimeout, no promises anywhere in the module — pure decisions in, effects out.
